questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 50 into the tree?  
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.  
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes  

![Red-Black Tree](https://storage.googleapis.com/exam-digitizer-images/rb_tree_50.png)
questions.explanation: When node 50 is inserted, it becomes the left child of 55. Since 55 is red, we have a double-red violation. The uncle of node 55 (the right child of 60) is a NIL node, which is considered black. The relationship (60 -> 55 -> 50) forms a 'line' (left child of a left child). In Red-Black tree insertion, when the uncle is black and the path forms a line, Case 3 is invoked (right rotate and recolor).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?  
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.  
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes  

![Red-Black Tree](https://storage.googleapis.com/exam-digitizer-images/rb_tree_10.png)
questions.explanation: Node 10 would be inserted as the left child of node 15. Since node 15 is black (bold), inserting a red node (10) underneath it does not violate any Red-Black tree properties. Therefore, no rebalancing cases are invoked.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the height of the tree?  
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.  
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes  

![Red-Black Tree](https://storage.googleapis.com/exam-digitizer-images/rb_tree_height.png)
questions.explanation: The height of a tree is the length of the longest path from the root to a leaf. If we include NIL nodes as leaves (as instructed), the longest path is 30 \(\rightarrow\) 45 \(\rightarrow\) 60 \(\rightarrow\) 55 \(\rightarrow\) NIL, which consists of 4 edges. Thus, the height is 4.

questions.text: The array \( \{25, 18, 19, 17, 14, 16, 10, 13, 21, 5, 12, 11, 7\} \) is not a max-heap, but the max-heap properties are violated only once. Which two numbers (parent and child) in the array violate the heap properties?
questions.explanation:
In a max-heap represented as an array, the children of node at index \( i \) (1-indexed) are at \( 2i \) and \( 2i+1 \).  
- Index 4 is 17. Its children are index 8 (13) and index 9 (21).  
- Since \( 21 > 17 \), the max-heap property (parent \( \ge \) children) is violated by the parent 17 and child 21.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, list the keys of the nodes using the pre-order traversal.  
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.  
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes  

![Red-Black Tree](https://storage.googleapis.com/exam-digitizer-images/rb_tree_traversal.png)
questions.explanation:
Pre-order traversal follows the Root \(\rightarrow\) Left \(\rightarrow\) Right pattern.  
1. Visit Root: 30  
2. Visit Left subtree of 30: 15  
3. Visit Right subtree of 30: 45  
   - Visit Left of 45: 35  
   - Visit Right of 45: 60  
     - Visit Left of 60: 55  
Sequence: 30, 15, 45, 35, 60, 55.

questions.text: If \( f(n) = 2 \) and \( g(n) = 10^{100} \), which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: Both \( f(n) \) and \( g(n) \) are constant functions. Asymptotically, any positive constant is equivalent to any other positive constant. Therefore, \( f(n) \) is \( O(g(n)) \), \( \Omega(g(n)) \), and \( \Theta(g(n)) \).

questions.text: Given the array \( A = \langle 11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 13, 9, 7 \rangle \), what is the state of the array after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm (Lomuto partition) one time?
questions.explanation:
Using Lomuto partition with pivot 7:  
- 11 > 7 (skip)  
- 10 > 7 (skip)  
- 1 < 7 (swap with 11): \( \langle 1, 10, 11, 6, 4, 13, 9, 7 \rangle \)  
- 6 < 7 (swap with 10): \( \langle 1, 6, 11, 10, 4, 13, 9, 7 \rangle \)  
- 4 < 7 (swap with 11): \( \langle 1, 6, 4, 10, 11, 13, 9, 7 \rangle \)  
- 13 > 7 (skip)  
- 9 > 7 (skip)  
- Final swap pivot with element after the low boundary (10): \( \langle 1, 6, 4, 7, 11, 13, 9, 10 \rangle \).

questions.text: Given an array \( A \) with the following nodes: \( 12, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15 \). After applying the `max-heapify` at node 2 (index 2 with value 30), the resulting array will be:
questions.explanation:
At index 2 (value 30), its children are index 4 (50) and index 5 (60).  
1. 60 is the largest, so swap 30 and 60. Array: 12, **60**, 40, 50, **30**, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15.  
2. Recurse at index 5 (value 30). Its children are index 10 (13) and index 11 (15).  
3. 30 is greater than its children, so heap property is satisfied. The process stops.

questions.text: The height \( h(x) \) of node \( x \) compared to its black-height \( bh(x) \) in a red black tree is:
questions.explanation: In a red-black tree, for any node \( x \), the path from \( x \) to a leaf has at least half of its nodes as black nodes. Specifically, the black height \( bh(x) \) satisfies \( bh(x) \ge h(x)/2 \), which implies \( h(x) \le 2bh(x) \).

questions.text: Let \( T(n) \) be a function obeying the recurrence \( T(n) = 9T(n / 9) + \log n \), then \( T(n) = \)
questions.explanation:
Using the Master Theorem: \( a=9, b=9, f(n)=\log n \).  
Calculate \( n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_9 9} = n^1 = n \).  
Since \( f(n) = \log n = O(n^{1-\epsilon}) \) for some \( \epsilon > 0 \), Case 1 of the Master Theorem applies.  
Therefore, \( T(n) = \Theta(n) \).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the successor of node 45?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

**Tree structure:**
- Root: **30**
- Left child of 30: **15**
- Right child of 30: <u>45</u>
- Left child of 45: **35**
- Right child of 45: **60**
- Left child of 60: <u>55</u>
questions.explanation: In a Binary Search Tree (BST), the in-order successor of a node is the node with the smallest key greater than the input node's key. For a node with a right subtree, this is the leftmost node in that right subtree. For node 45, the right subtree starts at 60, and its leftmost descendant is 55.

questions.text: Given a heap that contains 256 nodes, the height of the heap rooted at level 5 is:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree (heap) with 256 nodes has levels ranging from 0 to 8 (since \( 2^8 = 256 \)). The level of the root is 0. A node at level 5 is the root of a sub-heap. The maximum depth of the entire heap is 8. The height of the sub-heap is the distance from its root (level 5) to its deepest leaf (level 8), which is \( 8 - 5 = 3 \).

The `main` function contains a loop that runs \( n \) times, calling `XXX(i)` for each iteration. Inside `XXX(i)`, there is a loop that runs \( i \) times when \( i \ge 6 \). The total complexity is the sum of these inner loops: \( \sum_{i=6}^{n-1} i \). This is an arithmetic progression which evaluates to \( \frac{(n-1)n}{2} - constant \), resulting in a complexity of \( O(n^2) \).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data in sorted order in a binary search tree?
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits a Binary Search Tree in the order: Left Subtree, Root, Right Subtree. Since BST property ensures all values in the left subtree are smaller than the root and all values in the right subtree are larger, this traversal visits nodes in non-decreasing sorted order.

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \( T(n) = n^2 + 2T(n/3) \), and using the recursion tree method, what is the number of nodes in the tree at level \( i = 5 \)?
questions.explanation: In a recursion tree for \( T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) \), the number of nodes at any level \( i \) is \( a^i \). Here, \( a = 2 \). Therefore, at level \( i = 5 \), the number of nodes is \( 2^5 = 32 \).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree has a worst case height of:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree is always balanced. By definition, all levels except possibly the last are fully filled, and nodes in the last level are as far left as possible. The height of such a tree with \( n \) nodes is always \( \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the black height (bh) of node 45?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

**Tree structure:**
- Root: **30**
- Left child of 30: **15**
- Right child of 30: <u>45</u>
- Left child of 45: **35**
- Right child of 45: **60**
- Left child of 60: <u>55</u>
questions.explanation: Black height \( bh(x) \) is defined as the number of black nodes on any path from node \( x \) to a leaf (NIL node), not including \( x \) itself. From node 45 (which is red), one path is 45 \(\rightarrow\) **35** (black) \(\rightarrow\) NIL (black), giving a count of 2. All paths to leaves from 45 will contain exactly 2 black nodes.

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty binary heap: <10, 12, 1, 14, 6, 5, 8, 15, 3, 9, 7, 4, 11, 13, 2>. What data element is sitting in the right child of the left child of the root?
questions.explanation:
Following a standard level-order insertion for a complete binary tree (common interpretation for this type of exam question): 
- Level 0: 10 (Root)
- Level 1: 12 (Left child), 1 (Right child)
- Level 2: 14 (Left of 12), 6 (Right of 12), 5 (Left of 1), 8 (Right of 1)

The root is 10. Its left child is 12. The right child of 12 is 6.

questions.text: Given the array A = {13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6}, after running the loop in the insertion sort algorithm five times the result will be:

Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the depth of node 30?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

**Tree structure:**
- Root: **30**
- Left child of 30: **15**
- Right child of 30: <u>45</u>
- Left child of 45: **35**
- Right child of 45: **60**
- Left child of 60: <u>55</u>
questions.explanation: The depth of a node in a tree is the number of edges from the root to the node. Since node 30 is the root node, its depth is 0.

questions.text:
Based on the provided Red-Black tree, which case will be invoked when we insert node 50 into the tree?

**Tree structure:**
- **30** (Black, Root)
- **15** (Black, Left child of 30)
- _45_ (Red, Right child of 30)
- **35** (Black, Left child of 45)
- **60** (Black, Right child of 45)
- _55_ (Red, Left child of 60)

*Note: Bold nodes are black, underlined/italicized nodes are red. Don't forget NIL nodes.*
questions.explanation: When node 50 is inserted, it becomes the left child of node 55. Since node 55 is red, a double-red violation occurs. Node 50 (z) is the left child, its parent 55 (p) is the left child of 60 (g), and the uncle (right child of 60) is a NIL node (black). Because it is a straight-line configuration (left-left) and the uncle is black, this corresponds to **Case 3** in the Red-Black tree insertion algorithm.

questions.text:
Based on the provided Red-Black tree, which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?

**Tree structure:**
- **30** (Black, Root)
- **15** (Black, Left child of 30)
- _45_ (Red, Right child of 30)
- **35** (Black, Left child of 45)
- **60** (Black, Right child of 45)
- _55_ (Red, Left child of 60)
questions.explanation: When node 10 is inserted, it becomes the left child of node 15. Since node 15 is black, no Red-Black tree properties are violated. Therefore, no fix-up case is invoked.

questions.text: Based on the provided Red-Black tree, what is the height of the tree? (Consider NIL nodes as per the provided note).
questions.explanation: The height of a tree is the length of the longest path from the root to a leaf. Including the NIL nodes (as instructed), the longest path is 30 \(\rightarrow\) 45 \(\rightarrow\) 60 \(\rightarrow\) 55 \(\rightarrow\) NIL, which consists of 4 edges. Thus, the height is 4.

questions.text: Based on the provided Red-Black tree, list the keys of the nodes using the pre-order traversal.
questions.explanation:
Pre-order traversal visits the nodes in the order: Root, Left Subtree, Right Subtree. 
1. Visit Root: **30**
2. Visit Left child: **15**
3. Visit Right child: **45**
4. Visit Left child of 45: **35**
5. Visit Right child of 45: **60**
6. Visit Left child of 60: **55**
Resulting sequence: 30, 15, 45, 35, 60, 55.

questions.text: Based on the provided Red-Black tree, what is the successor of node 45?
questions.explanation: In a Binary Search Tree, the successor of a node is the node with the smallest key greater than the node's key. For node 45, we look at its right subtree (rooted at 60) and find the leftmost node. The leftmost node in the right subtree is 55.

questions.text: Based on the provided Red-Black tree, what is the black height (bh) of node 45?
questions.explanation:
Black height (bh) of a node is the number of black nodes on any path from that node to a leaf, not including the node itself. From node 45:
- Path 1: 45 \(\rightarrow\) 35 (Black) \(\rightarrow\) NIL (Black). Count = 2.
- Path 2: 45 \(\rightarrow\) 60 (Black) \(\rightarrow\) 55 (Red) \(\rightarrow\) NIL (Black). Count = 2.
Both paths yield a black height of 2.

questions.text: Based on the provided Red-Black tree, what is the depth of node 30?
questions.explanation: The depth of a node is the number of edges from the root to that node. Since node 30 is the root, its depth is 0.

questions.text: The array {25, 18, 19, 17, 14, 16, 10, 13, 21, 5, 12, 11, 7} is not a max-heap, but the max-heap properties are violated only once (involving numbers). Which two numbers (parent and child) in the array violate the heap properties?
questions.explanation: In a max-heap represented by an array (1-indexed), the children of the element at index \(i\) are at indices \(2i\) and \(2i+1\). The value 17 is at index 4. Its children are at index 8 (value 13) and index 9 (value 21). Since 21 > 17, the max-heap property (parent must be greater than or equal to children) is violated by the parent-child pair (17, 21).
Both \(f(n)\) and \(g(n)\) are constant functions. Asymptotically, all constant functions are equivalent. Therefore, \(f(n)\) is both \(O(g(n))\), \(\Omega(g(n))\), and \(\Theta(g(n))\).

questions.text: Given the array A <11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 13, 9, 7>, what is the resulting array after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm one time (using the last element as pivot)?
questions.explanation: Using the Lomuto partition scheme with 7 as the pivot: elements smaller than 7 (1, 6, 4) are moved to the left, then the pivot (7) is placed, followed by elements larger than 7 (11, 13, 9, 10). The resulting array order is 1, 6, 4, 7, 11, 13, 9, 10.

questions.text: Given an array A with the following nodes: 12, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15. After applying the max-heapify at node 2 (value 30) the result will be:
questions.explanation: Node 2 (value 30) has children at index 4 (50) and index 5 (60). The largest among {30, 50, 60} is 60. Swap 30 and 60. Now 30 is at index 5. Its children are at index 10 (13) and index 11 (15). Since 30 is larger than both, no further swaps are needed. The resulting array is 12, 60, 40, 50, 30, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15.

questions.text: The height \(h(x)\) of node \(x\) compared to its black-height \(bh(x)\) in a red-black tree is:
questions.explanation: A fundamental property of Red-Black Trees is that the height of any node \(x\) is at most twice its black-height: \(h(x) \le 2 bh(x)\).

questions.text: Let \(T(n)\) be a function obeying the recurrence \(T(n) = 9T(n/9) + \log n\), then \(T(n) = \)
questions.explanation: Using the Master Theorem: \(a=9, b=9, f(n)=\log n\). Comparing \(f(n)\) to \(n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_9 9} = n^1\). Since \(f(n) = \log n\) is polynomially smaller than \(n\) (i.e., Case 1), \(T(n) = \Theta(n)\).

questions.text: Given a heap that contains 256 nodes, the height of the sub-heap rooted at level 5 (assuming root is level 1) is:
questions.explanation: A heap with 256 nodes has a total height of 8 (since \(2^8 = 256\)). If the root is at level 1, nodes at level 5 are at depth 4. The height of a sub-tree starting at level 5 in a total depth of 8 is \(8 - 5 = 3\).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data in sorted order in a binary search tree?
questions.explanation: An inorder traversal (Left-Root-Right) of a Binary Search Tree (BST) visits nodes in non-decreasing (sorted) order.

The `main` function contains a loop that runs \(n\) times. In each iteration \(i\), it calls `XXX(i)`. The function `XXX(i)` has a loop that runs \(i\) times. The total complexity is the sum of \(i\) from 0 to \(n-1\), which is \(\sum i = n(n-1)/2\), resulting in \(O(n^2)\).

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \(T(n) = n^2 + 2 * T(n/3)\). Using the recursion tree method, what is the number of nodes in the tree at level \(i=5\)?
questions.explanation: In a recurrence of the form \(T(n) = a T(n/b) + f(n)\), the recursion tree has \(a^i\) nodes at level \(i\). Here, \(a=2\). For level \(i=5\), the number of nodes is \(2^5 = 32\).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree has a worst case height of:
questions.explanation: A **complete binary tree** of \( n \) nodes is always balanced by definition, meaning its height is \( \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor \). Since the structure must remain complete, even in the worst-case scenario for a binary search tree, the height will be \( O(\log n) \).

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty binary heap: \( <10, 12, 1, 14, 6, 5, 8, 15, 3, 9, 7, 4, 11, 13, 2> \). What data element is sitting in the right child of the left child of the root?
Given the array \( A = \{13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6\} \), after running the loop in the insertion sort algorithm five times the result will be:
questions.explanation:
Insertion sort builds a sorted sequence at the beginning of the array. Let's trace the state of the array after each of the first five iterations (keys processed from index 1 to 5):
- Initial: [13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]
- Iteration 1 (key=10): [10, 13, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]
- Iteration 2 (key=4): [4, 10, 13, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]
- Iteration 3 (key=19): [4, 10, 13, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]
- Iteration 4 (key=2): [2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6]
- Iteration 5 (key=30): [2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6]

After 5 iterations, the array is [2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6].
